comment.body:
for a daytrader it doesn't matter if the move is a bulltrap or not, just trade what is front of you.
for a swing trader it gives riskmanagement considerations, based on his portfolio... but he also plays the move...
This is a very unnecessary point to go on for daytrading, you just confuse yourselves again.

comment.body:
and once again: the risk is that market is extremely complacent about rate cuts, Trump and his team hypes this, vomma is sold, puts are sold, artificial liquidity.
Not a surprise, i said it before the weekly market opening ;))

Trump wants to mess with Powell... if datas don't show disinflation, and Fed communicates further wait-and-see, the whole leg down will be Powell's fault.
Fed Funds are up on NFP, not on disinflation. 
This is a redflag

comment.body:
for a daytrader, there is no point to buy puts on every move up.
for someone who holds longer-term positions, it is crucial to account for the risk, and hedge that, and be ready, until the risk is disproven.

the problem is that a lot of guys deal with things that are not their concern. you need to know your own strat
comment.raw_body:

comment.body:
also there is no point of always trying to frontrun the market, like if it would be some "who is right in advance" contest... thats a wrong midset.
trade the moves that are proven, place your bets based on probabilities, then size up the winners, cut the losers... thats it.
i wrote a whole educational post about that.

the success of a trader primarly depends on risk management, not on hit rate. but i wrote about this too in my monday post
